content.full_text:
BEST, BEST & KRIEGER
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING PROFESSIONAL CORPORATIONS

LAWYERS
400 MISSION SQUARE
3750 UNIVERSITY AVENUE
POST OFFICE BOX 1028
RIVERSIDE, CALIFORNIA 92502
TELEPHONE (714) 686-1450
TELECOPIER (714) 686-3083

PALM SPRINGS
(619) 325-7264

RANCHO MIRAGE
(619) 568-2611

ONTARIO
(714) 989-8584

July 25, 1990

Legal Department
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Sir or Madam:

We at Best, Best & Krieger represent a number of local
public entities around Southern California. One of our local
agency clients (the "Agency") has asked us whether a three-day
workshop/tour of a utility company's facilities hosted and paid
for by the utility company can qualify as a gift to the Agency
under the test set forth in In re Stone (1977) 3 FPPC Ops.52 and
recently reaffirmed in Opinion No. A-90-284. Because the
exemption in the general gift disclosure and disqualification
rules created by Stone has not been significantly elaborated on
by the FPPC or the Legislature, we hope you will allow us to
reaffirm our understanding of the Stone exception as it applies
to the Agency so that we may properly advise our client.

Our facts are as follows: A utility company has
offered to allow Agency officials and staff at any level to
participate in a tour/workshop of some of the utility company's
facilities in the Sierras. Expenses for the tour/workshop and
associated travel, accommodations and food will be paid for by
the utility company. The utility company has allowed the Agency
full discretion as to how the Agency will determine who will
participate in the tour/workshop. The Agency has adopted a
resolution (see attached) accepting the gift of the tour/workshop
on behalf of the Agency and authorizing the Agency's chair to
determine who will participate. The Agency's chair has
subsequently asked the Agency's Executive Director to go and
represent the Agency.

We believe that under the Stone opinion the Agency
official who participates in the tour/workshop will not be deemed
by the FPPC as having received a gift. Based on this conclusion,
we intend to advise our client that the disclosure and

LAW OFFICES OF

BEST, BEST & KRIEGER

Legal Department
FPPC
July 25, 1990
Page 2

disqualification requirements of the California Political Reform
Act do not require an official participating in that
tour/workshop to report the value of the tour/workshop or any
travel, accommodations, or food provided as part of the
tour/workshop, or to disqualify himself from any Agency actions
or deliberations regarding the utility company which may come
before the Commission at any time.

If our understanding of the Stone exemption is
incorrect, we ask you to so inform us so that we may advise our
client appropriately.

Very truly yours,

Steven C. DeBaun
for Best, Best & Krieger

SCD/des324

P.O. Box 1128
Nipomo, CA 93444
13 Aug 1990

County Board of Supervisors
County Government Center
San Luis Obispo, CA 93401

ATTN: Mrs. Delaney, Chairwomen County Board of Supervisors
Mr. Dvit, Member County Board of Supervisors
Mr. Coy, Member County Board of Supervisors
Mr. Johnson, Member County Board of Supervisors
Mr. Blakley, Member County Board of Supervisors
Mr. Lindholm, County Counsel
Mrs. Shelby, Clerk County Board of Supervisors

RE: Citizens For Honest Representation Letters dated 10 Jul & 11
Jul 1990 regarding Mr. Johnson's conflicts of interest.

Dear San Luis Obispo Board of Supervisors and County Counsel:

Mr. Johnson has had a conflict of interest from the first time
the board discussed growth management/limitation measures in 1989.
His flagrant disregard for the law places the taxpayers at
tremendous financial risk. Even after being challenged by our 10
July 1990 letter, neither the County Counsel or any of the Board of
Supervisors challenged Johnson to step down. By virtue of the fact
that each of you were made aware of Johnson's conflicts of interest
and you allowed him to participate in your actions, the matter is
no longer limited to Johnson's liability. Each of you may now be
considered, jointly or unwittingly, co-conspirators and face
sanctions under the Government Code. Johnson's breaking of the law
is now your problem too.

Johnson even admitted at the 17 July 1990 Board of Supervisors
meeting that he has a conflict of interest by stepping aside during
the proposed growth control ordinance permit discussion, but
publicly admitted he has eliminated his conflicts of interest. We
believe Johnson should make public the documentation that relieves
him of his conflicts of interest.

You as a group, by not challenging Johnson, have made the
taxpayers liable for multi-millions of dollars in law suits.
County counsel we feel you are remiss in your duties for not
properly representing the taxpayers in this matter.

Request any and all of you take what ever action is necessary
to preclude Johnson's further participation and/or voting on any
pending growth management/limitation ordinance to prevent
additional law suits against the taxpayers. Enough is enough.

Citizens for Honest Representation

George Layman
(805)489-6011

cc. FPPC - Attn: Mr. McLean
SLO County DA - Attn: Mr. Barry LaBarbera
